When there are two or more feet in a word or phrase, in
general, the rightmost foot gets a primary stress. Therefore,
many English speakers would feel that ‚Äėteen‚Äô is stronger than
‚Äėfour‚Äô in ‚Äėfourteen.‚Äô Another important tendency of English
stress is that speech carries a regular alternation between
stronger and weaker units. When ‚Äėfourteen men‚Äô is spoken in
conversational style, the primary stress of ‚Äėfourteen‚Äô is
adjusted. Since ‚Äėmen‚Äô gets the primary stress of the whole
phrase, ‚Äėfour‚Äô is pronounced more strongly than ‚Äėteen‚Äô to
avoid the clash of two lexical primary stresses.

As exemplified in (1), the secondary and the primary
stresses of a word are reversed when it is followed by another
word. This is called ‚Äėstress-shift‚Äô or ‚Äėrhythm reversal.‚Äô
Although English speakers have a strong tendency to use a
regular rhythm in their speech, it is not the case that this
stress-shift takes place whenever the two feet containing a
lexical primary stress are adjacent. Look at the data in (2).
